confidence.disclosure: Sir Muhammad Iqbal, the great Urdu and Persian poet-philosopher and the spiritual father of Pakistan, is exceptionally well documented through his own letters, the biographical writings of his contemporaries (especially Atiya Faizi and Abdul Majid Salik), and the extensive scholarship that has built up since his death. The major events of his life - birth in Sialkot, education at Lahore and Cambridge, doctorate at Munich, return to India, his major works, the famous Allahabad address of 1930, and his death in Lahore - are firm by year and many by day.

sources: Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam (1930)
Annemarie Schimmel, Gabriel's Wing: A Study into the Religious Ideas of Sir Muhammad Iqbal (1963)

journey.event: Born on 9 November 1877 in Sialkot in the Punjab (then in British India, now in Pakistan), into a Kashmiri Brahmin family that had converted to Islam four or five generations earlier. His father Shaykh Nur Muhammad is a tailor of modest means but deeply religious. Iqbal grows up speaking Urdu and Punjabi.

Studies at Scotch Mission College in Sialkot, where his teacher Mawlana Mir Hasan, a deeply learned scholar of classical Arabic and Persian, becomes the central formative influence on the young Iqbal. From Mir Hasan he learns the classical Persian poetic tradition - Rumi, Hafez, Saadi, Bedil - that will shape his own poetry.

In 1895 moves to Lahore, the cultural capital of the Punjab, to enrol at Government College Lahore. Studies philosophy and English literature, eventually taking an MA in philosophy in 1899. Becomes a student and protege of the British orientalist Thomas Arnold, who teaches philosophy at the college.

After his MA, takes a position teaching philosophy and English at the Oriental College of the University of the Punjab in Lahore. Also begins to gain a reputation as a poet at the literary mushairas of Lahore, reciting Urdu ghazals in a style that mixes traditional themes with modern social and political concerns.

In September 1905 sails for England to continue his philosophical studies. Enrols at Trinity College, Cambridge, on the advice of his teacher Thomas Arnold (who has now returned to England). Studies Hegel, Bergson, Nietzsche, and McTaggart with the leading philosophers of the day. Spends three formative years in Europe.

Moves to Munich to take a doctorate at the Ludwig Maximilian University. Submits a thesis on The Development of Metaphysics in Persia, written in English, which he defends successfully in 1907. The thesis is the first systematic Western study of Persian Islamic philosophy and remains in print to this day.

After Munich returns to London, where he is called to the Bar at Lincoln's Inn in 1908. Practises briefly as a lawyer in London. During his European years he experiences a deep intellectual crisis: he comes to reject the ideas of Persian pantheism and the passive Sufism that he had earlier embraced, and to advocate instead the active, self-affirming concept of khudi (selfhood) that becomes central to his later philosophy.
In late 1908 returns to Lahore. Sets up a legal practice (which he conducts somewhat reluctantly to support his family) and resumes teaching part-time. Lives in Lahore for the rest of his life.

In 1915 publishes his first major Persian poetic work, the Asrar-i Khudi (Secrets of the Self), a long mathnawi expounding the philosophy of khudi - the active, creative self - as the foundation of human existence and Islamic renewal. The book causes a sensation; it is followed in 1918 by the Rumuz-i Bekhudi (Mysteries of Selflessness) on the relation of the self to the community.

In 1922 is knighted by the British government for his literary achievements, becoming Sir Muhammad Iqbal. He continues to publish prolifically in Persian and Urdu - the Payam-i Mashriq (Message of the East, 1923) is his answer to Goethe's West-östlicher Diwan; the Bang-i Dara (Call of the Caravan-Bell, 1924) collects his Urdu poems.

In 1928 and 1929 delivers six lectures at Madras, Hyderabad, and Aligarh that are published in 1930 as The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam, his most important prose work. In English, they argue for a creative reinterpretation of Islamic thought in the light of modern science, philosophy, and the experience of the modern world.

On 29 December 1930 delivers his famous presidential address at the annual session of the All-India Muslim League at Allahabad, in which he calls for 'the formation of a consolidated North-West Indian Muslim state' as the destiny of the Muslims of India. The speech is later regarded as the first authoritative articulation of the demand for what will become Pakistan.

In 1931 and 1932 attends the Second and Third Round Table Conferences in London, representing Indian Muslim interests in the constitutional negotiations between the British government and Indian leaders. While in Europe he meets the philosopher Henri Bergson in Paris and visits Spain (where he writes his famous poem on the mosque of Cordoba) and Italy.

Visits the Mezquita of Cordoba in 1932 - the first Muslim allowed to pray inside the great mosque-cathedral in modern times. The visit inspires his famous Urdu poem Masjid-i Qurtuba (The Mosque of Cordoba), one of the masterpieces of his late Urdu poetry, in which the historic mosque becomes a meditation on the relations between time, eternity, and Islamic civilisation.

From 1934 his health begins to deteriorate seriously. He suffers from a throat ailment that makes speaking and reciting poetry increasingly painful. Despite this he continues to write; the great Persian work Javid-nama (1932), modelled on Dante's Divine Comedy and tracing a journey through the spheres in the company of Rumi, is his philosophical-poetic testament.

Dies in his house in Lahore on 21 April 1938 (20 Safar 1357 AH), at about five in the morning, aged 60. His last verses, dictated to a friend just hours before his death, end with the famous lines: 'Whether new melodies will come or not, who knows; whether the breeze from Hijaz will blow again or not, who knows.' He is buried beside the great Badshahi Mosque in Lahore, where his tomb (built between 1946 and 1951) stands today and is visited by millions. Pakistan, founded nine years after his death, regards him as its spiritual founder and national poet.
